title: Matthew
author: Matthew (Levi), tax collector and apostle of Jesus
setting: Written primarily for a Jewish-Christian audience, demonstrating that Jesus is the promised Messiah and the fulfillment of all that the OT anticipated.
purpose: To present Jesus as the Jewish Messiah, the Son of David, the Son of Abraham, in whom all the promises of the OT are fulfilled. Matthew's distinctive emphasis on the Kingdom of Heaven and the five great discourses (chs. 5–7, 10, 13, 18, 24–25) structures Jesus' teaching as a new Pentateuch.
themes: Jesus as the new Moses and greater David
Discipleship and the cost of following

outline.label: Discourse 1: The Sermon on the Mount
Peter's Confession and the Road to Jerusalem

key_people.name: Jesus Christ
key_people.role: The central figure of the book — Son of David, Son of Abraham, Immanuel, and the Son of God whose teaching, miracles, death, and resurrection constitute the new and greater Torah

key_people.name: Matthew (Levi)
key_people.role: The tax collector called by Jesus (9:9) who is traditionally identified as the author; his background as a record-keeper likely accounts for the book's meticulous structure and use of OT fulfilment texts

key_people.name: John the Baptist
key_people.role: The Elijah-figure who prepares Jesus' way, baptizes him, and whose imprisonment and death foreshadow Jesus' own fate

key_people.name: Peter
key_people.role: The disciple who receives the keys of the kingdom (16:19) and whose confession at Caesarea Philippi is the book's theological turning point

key_people.name: The Pharisees and Scribes
key_people.role: The primary antagonists whose growing opposition to Jesus structures the second half of the book; their hardness of heart is a warning to Matthew's own community

context: Matthew was written c. AD 80–90, probably in Syrian Antioch, for a Jewish-Christian community navigating its relationship to its Jewish heritage after the destruction of the temple (AD 70). The book is deeply rooted in OT Scripture — containing more direct OT citations than any other gospel — and structured around five great teaching discourses that parallel the five books of Moses. Its primary concern is to show that Jesus is the fulfilment of all OT expectation.
christ_connection: Matthew is the most explicitly fulfilment-focused gospel, with ten signature formula-quotations ('this happened to fulfil what was spoken by the prophet...'). Jesus is presented as the new Moses (the five discourses parallel the Pentateuch), the new Israel (recapitulating Israel's history), Son of David (opening genealogy), Immanuel (1:23; 28:20 form an inclusion), and the Son in whom all sonship is perfectly realised. The Sermon on the Mount (chs. 5-7) is Jesus' authoritative exposition of the Torah's true meaning — not abolishing but fulfilling.

key_verses.note: 'Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and they shall call his name Immanuel (which means, God with us)' — the book's opening christological declaration, fulfilled in 28:20
'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfil them' — Jesus' programmatic statement about his relationship to OT Scripture
'You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' — the founding declaration of the church
The Great Commission: all authority given to Christ, the command to disciple all nations, and the promise of his presence to the end of the age — the book's climactic conclusion

themes_detail.title: Jesus as the Fulfillment of the OT
themes_detail.text: Matthew's formula quotations are not proof-texts plucked from context but arguments that Jesus' life, death, and resurrection are the events toward which the entire OT narrative was pointing. He quotes from the Law, Prophets, and Writings, covering events as small as the flight to Egypt (2:15) and as large as the crucifixion (27:46). The point is not merely that isolated verses were predicted but that the whole OT story — of God dwelling with his people, of exodus and exile, of covenant and king — finds its resolution in the person of Jesus.

themes_detail.title: The Kingdom of Heaven
themes_detail.text: Matthew uses the phrase 'kingdom of heaven' (a Jewish circumlocution for 'kingdom of God') more than any other gospel writer — 32 times. The kingdom is both present (it has come near, 4:17; is being entered, 5:20) and future (its coming is prayed for, 6:10; it will be inherited at the end, 25:34). Its character is paradoxical: it belongs to the poor in spirit and the persecuted (5:3, 10), advances without military force, and its citizens are those who do the Father's will. The parables of the kingdom (ch. 13) are Matthew's most sustained exploration of this paradox.

themes_detail.title: Jesus as Teacher and the New Torah
themes_detail.text: The five discourses (chs. 5-7, 10, 13, 18, 24-25) are Matthew's structural backbone and parallel the Pentateuch. Jesus teaches 'as one who had authority, and not as their scribes' (7:29) — not citing precedent but speaking in the first person ('But I say to you'). The Sermon on the Mount is not a relaxation of the law's demands (the righteousness required exceeds that of the Pharisees, 5:20) but an exposition of what the law always meant when understood in terms of its inner intention rather than its external form. Jesus is the true and final teacher of Israel.

themes_detail.title: The Mission to the Nations
themes_detail.text: Matthew opens with Gentile magi worshipping the infant Jesus and closes with the commission to disciple all nations — framing the entire book as a universal mission story. Within the book, Jesus ministers to a Canaanite woman (15:21-28), heals a centurion's servant (8:5-13), and declares the centurion's faith greater than any in Israel. The parable of the tenants (21:43) warns that the kingdom will be taken from unfaithful Israel and given to 'a people producing its fruits' — the multinational church. The Great Commission is not an addendum but the logical conclusion of Matthew's christology.
